items.desc: Hosting fundraising events at home, sponsoring community galas ($5K-25K), endowing annual scholarships, or making significant year-end charitable gifts ($10K-50K+). Named sponsorships at children's schools or local organizations.
items.financialNote: Philanthropy becomes intertwined with social life and celebrations.

items.title: Event Production-Scale Celebrations
items.desc: Celebrations managed by top event planners (Colin Cowie, David Stark) with budgets of $50K-500K+. Custom venues, celebrity entertainment, Michelin-starred catering. International guest lists with chartered transportation. Documentary-quality photography and videography.
items.financialNote: Celebrations are produced events comparable to celebrity weddings.

items.title: Transformational Philanthropy & Naming Gifts
items.desc: Major naming gifts to hospitals, universities, or cultural institutions ($1M-10M+). Establishing family foundations with professional staff. Holiday season marked by significant charitable distributions and gala fundraising events.
items.financialNote: Giving at a level that creates lasting institutional change.

items.title: Legacy-Defining Celebrations & Global Philanthropy
items.desc: Events that make international headlines. Wedding budgets of $1M-10M+ (private island buyouts, international guest logistics). Philanthropic gifts that establish or rename institutions. Holiday giving programs that affect thousands of beneficiaries through family foundation.
items.financialNote: Celebrations and giving at a scale that shapes communities and institutions.

items.title: Family, Church & Free Community Events
items.desc: Social life centers on family gatherings, religious community (church potlucks, youth groups), and free local events (community festivals, library programs). Socializing at home or in public parks. Limited ability to participate in paid social activities.
items.financialNote: Social budget effectively $0. Relationships maintained through proximity, shared activities, and community institutions.

items.title: Limited Social Spending (~$0-20/month)
items.desc: Can rarely afford to go out with friends. Social media and phone calls replace in-person socializing when transportation or venue costs are barriers. May skip events due to inability to split checks or buy rounds.
items.financialNote: Social isolation can be a hidden cost of poverty.

items.title: Budget Social Outings & Community Involvement (~$30-60/month)
items.desc: Occasional drinks with friends at dive bars ($15-25/outing), potluck dinner parties, backyard BBQs. Joining a recreational sports league ($50-100/season: softball, volleyball, bowling). Church or community group involvement. Budget dating: coffee dates ($5-10), walks, free museum days.
items.financialNote: Social budget $30-60/month. Creative socializing that doesn't break the bank.

items.title: Online Community & Social Media Engagement
items.desc: Active in online communities (Reddit, Facebook groups, Discord) related to hobbies and interests. Social media as a primary tool for maintaining friendships and staying connected. Online dating apps (free tiers: Tinder, Hinge, Bumble).
items.financialNote: Digital social life supplements limited in-person social budget.

items.title: Regular Social Outings & Group Activities (~$100-200/month)
items.desc: Regular happy hours with coworkers ($20-40/outing, 2-3x/month). Casual dinner parties at home with friends (hosting $50-100). Joining local clubs: book clubs, running groups, parent groups. Weekend activities with friends: hiking, movies ($15-20), bowling ($20-30).
items.financialNote: Social life is active and varied. Budget $100-200/month for socializing.

items.title: Community Memberships & Volunteering
items.desc: Joining community organizations: PTA ($10-25/year), local volunteer groups, neighborhood associations. Basic gym membership doubles as social outlet (Planet Fitness $10-25/mo, YMCA $30-50/mo). Coaching kids' sports teams.
items.financialNote: Community involvement builds social network and creates belonging.

items.title: Dating & Couple Social Life (~$100-200/month)
items.desc: Comfortable dating: dinners out ($40-80), drinks ($20-40), movies, activities. Paid dating apps (Hinge+ $35/mo, Bumble Premium $40/mo). Double dates and couples' game nights. Splitting costs with partner.
items.financialNote: Dating budget manageable. Mix of going out and hosting at home.

items.title: Active Social Calendar & Memberships (~$200-400/month)
items.desc: Regular dinners out with friends ($50-100/person, 3-4x/month). Wine clubs, supper clubs, or dining groups. Weekend brunches ($25-50). Hosting cocktail parties and themed dinners at home ($200-500/event). Membership in professional networking organizations (Chamber of Commerce, industry groups).
items.financialNote: Social spending $200-400/month. Social life is well-funded and varied.

items.title: Upscale Leisure & Social Clubs
items.desc: Joining mid-tier social/fitness clubs: Equinox ($200-300/mo), upscale golf club (semi-private $200-500/mo), tennis or racquet club ($100-300/mo). These serve dual purpose as fitness and social networking.
items.financialNote: Club memberships as a social networking investment.

items.title: Premium Social Scene & Private Club Memberships (~$500-1,500/month)
items.desc: Members-only social clubs: Soho House ($3,600/year), The Battery (SF $2,400/year), or local equivalents. Country club membership ($5K-25K initiation + $300-800/mo dues). Regular attendance at charity galas ($250-500/ticket), museum openings, and cultural events. Hosting substantial dinner parties ($500-1,500/event).
items.financialNote: Social spending $500-1,500/month. Social network becomes a strategic asset for career and lifestyle.

items.title: Networking Events & Industry Leadership
items.desc: Speaking at or sponsoring industry conferences ($1,000-5,000 for sponsorship tiers). Attending exclusive networking events and private dinners with industry leaders. Board membership at non-profits ($500-5,000/year in expected giving). Political fundraising events ($500-2,800/person for candidates).
items.financialNote: Social activities blur with professional advancement and community influence.

items.title: Upscale Dating & Social Experiences
items.desc: Premium dating platforms: Raya (by invitation), The League ($350/year), or matchmaking services ($5K-25K). High-end date experiences: private dining, box seats at events, weekend getaways. Significant wedding guest budgets (gifts $200-500, destination weddings $2K-5K for attendance).
items.financialNote: Dating and social experiences at a premium level.

items.title: Elite Club Memberships & Social Leadership (~$2,000-5,000/month)
items.desc: Multiple exclusive club memberships: private golf clubs (Augusta waitlist, Pebble Beach Club $250K+ initiation), urban social clubs (Core Club NYC $50K initiation, The Arts Club London £2,500/year). Chairing charity events and galas. Hosting fundraisers at home for political candidates or causes ($10K-50K events).
items.financialNote: Club dues and social obligations $25K-60K+/year. Social position is carefully cultivated.

items.title: Philanthropic & Civic Leadership
items.desc: Board seats at major non-profits, hospitals, or cultural institutions (expected giving $10K-100K+/year). Hosting salons and private gatherings for thought leaders. Significant political engagement: bundling donations, hosting candidates. Recognized community leader.
items.financialNote: Social influence wielded through philanthropy, civic engagement, and network curation.

items.title: Ultra-Exclusive Networks & Global Social Capital
items.desc: Membership in the most exclusive global clubs and networks: YPO/WPO, Tiger 21 ($30K/year), invitation-only events like Allen & Company Sun Valley Conference, Bilderberg, or Davos (World Economic Forum). Personal relationships with heads of state, CEOs, and cultural figures. Hosting intimate gatherings that shape industries or policy.
items.financialNote: Social capital is among the most valuable assets. Access and influence at the highest levels.

items.title: Major Philanthropic Leadership & Legacy Projects
items.desc: Founding or co-chairing major philanthropic initiatives. Signing The Giving Pledge or equivalent. Philanthropy teams managed by family office. Named buildings, programs, and endowed positions across multiple institutions.
items.financialNote: Social leadership expressed through transformational giving and institution-building.

items.title: Shaping Global Discourse & Institution Building
items.desc: Founding think tanks, media organizations, or cultural institutions. Convening global leaders for private summits. Significant political influence through Super PACs, lobbying, and direct relationships. Social circle includes world leaders, Nobel laureates, and global business titans.
items.financialNote: Social influence operates at a geopolitical scale. Every social interaction has strategic implications.

items.title: Dynastic Social Networks & Legacy Cultivation
items.desc: Multigenerational social networks cultivated across old-money families. Membership in century-old social institutions. Children introduced to peer networks through exclusive schools, camps, and social events designed to build lifelong connections.
items.financialNote: Social network is a dynastic asset, carefully maintained across generations.
